content.text: before answering, dramatically reducing hallucination and making verification easy in long-context tasks.
"For long documents, ask the model to quote relevant passages before answering. This forces it to locate evidence first, rather than generating from a vague impression of the content."
"Grounding is a prompt engineering technique used in LLMs where the user provides specific and relevant context within the prompt."

"Infuses LLM prompts with your internal data — including structured data (Excel spreadsheets, CRM data) and unstructured data (PDFs, chat logs, email messages, blog posts) — 'grounding' the prompt in relevant context."
"Copying and pasting relevant data directly into the prompt's context window."
"Grounding dramatically reduces hallucination. If the model can't find a relevant quote, that's a useful signal — the answer might not be in the document."

— Model must find quotes before answering
-
— If no quote found, answer might not exist
"This forces it to locate evidence first, rather than generating from a vague impression of the content."

- Model generates from vague impression
- Risk of hallucination
- Hard to verify

- Model locates exact quotes
- Evidence-based answer
- Easy to verify
"Ask for quotes first — grounding reduces hallucination and makes verification easy."
"First, extract the exact quotes from the contract that relate to termination clauses. Place them in
tags. Then, based only on those quotes, summarize the termination conditions in plain language."

<document>
{{CONTRACT_TEXT}}
</document>

First, extract the exact quotes from the contract that relate to
termination clauses. Place each quote in <quote> tags with:
- The exact text
- The section number
- The page number (if available)

<quotes>
<quote>
text: "The agreement may be terminated by either party..."
section: "Section 12.3"
page: "Page 45"
</quote>

<quote>
text: "Termination shall take effect 30 days after notice..."
section: "Section 12.4"
page: "Page 45"
</quote>
</quotes>

Then, based ONLY on those quotes, summarize the termination
conditions in plain language.
"Quote-First Analysis — Ask Claude to quote relevant parts of the documents first before carrying out its task. Then based on that quoted text, ask specific questions in the prompt."

<document>
{{FULL_DOCUMENT_TEXT}}
</document>

STEP 1: Extract Quotes
Find and extract all quotes that mention "revenue growth".
For each quote:
- Include exact text
- Note page/section
- Explain why it's relevant

STEP 2: Answer Questions
Based ONLY on the quotes from Step 1:
1. What are the revenue growth projections?
2. How do expenses compare to last quarter?
3. What risks are mentioned?
"Label your sources — indexed, tagged documents let the model cite where each claim comes from."

<documents>
<document index="1">
<source>Q3 Earnings Report</source>
<content>{{EARNINGS_REPORT_TEXT}}</content>
</document>

<document index="2">
<source>Competitor Analysis</source>
<content>{{COMPETITOR_ANALYSIS_TEXT}}</content>
</document>
</documents>

Find all quotes about revenue growth.
For each quote:
- Include exact text
- Cite document index
- Cite page/section

<quotes>
<quote source="document_1">
text: "The company projected 15% revenue growth for Q4."
source: Q3 Earnings Report, Page 12
</quote>

<quote source="document_2">
text: "Competitor X launched similar product."
source: Competitor Analysis, Page 8
</quote>
</quotes>

Compare revenue growth between both documents.
"I am using GPT 4 Turbo to extract quotations from a large document. My goal is to find all quotations that match criteria specified in my prompt."

<document>
{{FULL_DOCUMENT_TEXT}}
</document>

Criteria for quotes:
- Must mention "customer satisfaction"
- Must include a metric or percentage
- Must be from Q3 or Q4 sections

Find all quotes matching these criteria:

<quotes>
<quote>
text: "Our customer satisfaction score improved to 92% in Q3."
section: "Q3 Results"
page: "Page 15"
matches_criteria: "Yes - mentions customer satisfaction, includes metric, from Q3"
</quote>

<quote>
text: "We achieved 95% satisfaction in Q4, exceeding our target."
section: "Q4 Highlights"
page: "Page 22"
matches_criteria: "Yes - mentions customer satisfaction, includes metric, from Q4"
</quote>
</quotes>
"Maybe ask the LLM to generate a score from 1 to 100 of how likely each paragraph is to match your criteria and then you make the cutoff yourself."

<document>
{{FULL_DOCUMENT_TEXT}}
</document>

For each paragraph that mentions "innovation":
1. Extract the exact quote
2. Score how well it matches (1-100)
3. Note why it matches

Found paragraphs:

<quotes>
<quote score="95">
text: "Our innovation strategy focuses on AI-driven solutions."
score_reason: "Directly mentions innovation, specific about AI focus"
</quote>

<quote score="80">
text: "We are committed to innovation in all areas."
score_reason: "Mentions innovation but vague"
</quote>

<quote score="60">
text: "The company will continue to innovate."
score_reason: "Generic mention, no specifics"
</quote>
</quotes>

Select all quotes with score >= 70.
"So breaking into smaller chunks is good."

Chunk 1 (Pages 1-50):
<document index="1" part="1">
{{FIRST_CHUNK_TEXT}}
</document>

Chunk 2 (Pages 51-100):
<document index="1" part="2">
{{SECOND_CHUNK_TEXT}}
</document>

Chunk 3 (Pages 101-150):
<document index="1" part="3">
{{THIRD_CHUNK_TEXT}}
</document>

For each chunk, extract quotes about "revenue":

<quotes>
<quote chunk="1">
text: "Revenue increased by 15%."
page: "Page 25"
</quote>

<quote chunk="2">
text: "Q3 revenue reached $5M."
page: "Page 75"
</quote>
</quotes>
"Static grounding: Facts embedded directly in the prompt. Good for stable, small fact sets (brand guidelines, product specs, common policies)."

<grounding_context>
Brand Guidelines:
- Tone: Professional but accessible
- No jargon without explanation
- Include 2-3 practical examples

Product Specs:
- Free tier: 1,000 tokens/month
- Pro tier: 100,000 tokens/month
- Enterprise: Custom pricing
</grounding_context>

<document>
{{DOCUMENT_TEXT}}
</document>

Based ONLY on the grounding context above, write a marketing
email for the Pro tier.
"Dynamic grounding (RAG): Facts retrieved per-query from a vector DB or search index. Good for large or frequently-changing knowledge (docs, support articles, internal wikis)."

<grounding_context>
[Retrieved from vector DB based on query]

Document 1: "Customer satisfaction improved to 92% in Q3."
Document 2: "Revenue growth projections for Q4: 15%."
Document 3: "Operating expenses increased by 8%."
</grounding_context>

Using ONLY the retrieved context above, answer:
"What are the key Q3-Q4 metrics?"
"Tool-call grounding: Model calls a function to fetch facts on demand (database lookup, API call). Good for exact-answer data (account balances, inventory counts)."

Available tools:
- get_account_balance(user_id)
- get_order_details(order_id)
- get_stock_quote(ticker)

User query: "What's my account balance?"

Model should:
1. Call get_account_balance(user_id="12345")
2. Return result: "$1,234.56"
3. Ground answer in exact result
"Add an explicit rule: 'If the answer isn't in the provided context, say you don't know. Never guess.'"

<document>
{{DOCUMENT_TEXT}}
</document>

RULE: If the answer isn't in the provided context, say "I don't know."
Never guess or hallucinate.

Question: What is the termination clause?

<quotes>
<quote>
text: "The agreement may be terminated by either party..."
section: "Section 12.3"
</quote>
</quotes>

Answer: [Based only on quote above]
"Put facts before the task. Model sees context first, task second."
, or markdown sections. The model is better at 'refer to the context' when context is visibly delimited."
"Don't pad. Irrelevant context degrades quality (middle-forgetting effect; attention dilution). If a fact isn't useful for this query, leave it out."

"Chunking affects everything. Chunk size, overlap, and whether you chunk by fixed length or semantic boundary (paragraph, section) — all impact quality. Default to semantic chunks at paragraph or section level."
"Re-rank. After initial retrieval, run a cross-encoder re-ranker to pick the best subset. Easy +10-20% relevance."
"Top-K is a calibration choice. Retrieve too few — miss relevant context. Retrieve too many — dilute the prompt. Start at 5."
"First, extract the exact quotes... Place them in <quotes> tags."
"Then, based ONLY on those quotes, summarize the termination conditions in plain language."
"Grounding dramatically reduces hallucination. If the model can't find a relevant quote, that's a useful signal — the answer might not be in the document."
